title: No One Is Alone in the Family of God
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 12-10-23 -AM- No One is Alone in the Family of God - Ephesians 3_14-19 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: The Problem: Loneliness and Darkness During the Christmas Season
subpoints: Many people experience increased loneliness and depression in December
Christians may feel guilt on top of their loneliness
The solution is found in what the incarnation of God the Son accomplished

outline.title: Christ Has Brought You Into His Family
subpoints: God is the Father of the whole family in heaven and earth (Eph. 3:14-15)
God personally adopted each believer and gave them His name
Adoption was planned from eternity — not a rash decision
The Father's adoption of us brought Him great pleasure (Eph. 1:5)

outline.title: The Father's Blessings Are Immeasurably Rich
subpoints: Resources of God's blessings are nothing less than the infinity of God (Eph. 3:16)
God strengthens us at the deepest level — in the inner man by the Spirit
Christ continually dwells in us through His Spirit (Eph. 3:17)
Proof of Christ dwelling in us: increasing conformity to Him

outline.title: The Greatest Blessing: The Love of Christ
subpoints: Paul prays that the family of God would comprehend the width, length, depth, and height of Christ's love
The love of Christ passes knowledge — ten million years from now we will not have exhausted it
Eighteen aspects of the love of Christ identified in the New Testament
The result: being filled with all the fullness of God (Eph. 3:19)

outline.title: Conclusion: You Are Not Alone
subpoints: No Christian is objectively alone — God has brought us into one family
Your Father is ready to fill you to the brim with Himself
The birth of Christ is the declaration of this love

summary: In this Christmas morning sermon, Pastor Lyro addresses the loneliness and depression many people experience during the holiday season, particularly those whose family situations are marked by brokenness or loss. He argues that the incarnation of God the Son answers this darkness with one profound truth: Christ has brought believers into His family, and the Father will never leave or forsake them. Drawing from Ephesians 3:14-21, Pastor Lyro explores three dimensions of the Father's blessing to His adopted children. First, God has personally and lovingly brought every believer into a family more numerous than the stars, planned from eternity — not a rash decision made in the excitement of Christmas, but a carefully ordered act of sovereign grace. Second, the Father strengthens His children at the deepest level — in the inner man — through the Holy Spirit, and sends His Son to dwell in their hearts. Third and most magnificently, the Father loves His children with a love that passes all knowledge: a love so wide, long, deep, and high that ten billion years from now we will not have come close to exhausting it. The sermon lists eighteen aspects of the love of Christ found throughout the New Testament and concludes that the goal of all this blessing is that believers "may be filled with all the fullness of God." Whatever their circumstances, no Christian is objectively alone this Christmas season — their Father is ready to fill them, and the household of God is here to love them.
transcript:
## Introduction: Loneliness and the Christmas Season

There are very few people who are completely indifferent to the holiday season. A lot of people love it — time off from work, presents, extra time with family, food, eggnog, special church services. The Hallmark Channel got it right: the magic of Christmas — everybody stands to buy into that.

For quite a few people, however, the holiday season is not magical at all. It can often be a time of increased loneliness. It can be a time when the memories of broken relationships become more powerful. Even the rate of depression in the United States peaks in December more than any other time of the year. For Christians, the loneliness and depression are often accompanied by a sense of guilt and embarrassment, because everyone around them seems to be happy — and they are not.

Often this darkness that comes over the soul during the Christmas season is related to family: a father who drank through the holiday season, kids who no longer speak to mom or dad, a death or rejection that happened during this time of year. Those who don't believe in Jesus Christ have no real hope of deliverance from the darkness of the soul unless God changes their hearts.

For those who have entrusted their souls to Jesus Christ, the very thing we are celebrating this time of year is the light that shines on our souls and dispels the darkness of despair and discouragement. So why does the incarnation of God the Son dispel the darkness? There are many answers, but I want to look at just one: Christ has brought you into His family, and the Father will never leave you or forsake you.

## You Are Brought Into the Family of God

Paul says in Ephesians 3:14-15: "For this reason I bow my knees to the Father of our Lord Jesus Christ, from whom the whole family in heaven and earth is named, that He would grant you according to the riches of His glory to be strengthened with might through His Spirit in the inner man."

The Holy Spirit identifies God as the Father of the whole family in heaven and on earth. You are part of a family that is more numerous than the stars of heaven, the grains of sand on the seashore, and the specks of dust on the earth. You are part of a family whose members are spread all over the earth.

God has personally adopted you and added you into His family. He didn't name you in bulk — He thought of your name as a child of God and gave it to you precisely for you. That's what God has done for us. The Father, through the Lord Jesus Christ, adopted us as His own children. As Paul says in Ephesians 1: "Having predestined us to adoption as sons by Jesus Christ to Himself, according to the good pleasure of His will, to the praise of the glory of His grace, by which He made us accepted in the Beloved."

Adopting you, adopting me into His family brought Him great pleasure. His adoption of us wasn't a rash decision made in the excitement of the birth of His Son in Bethlehem. It was carefully planned from all eternity that through the incarnation of Jesus Christ you would be adopted and given the name "child of God."

As the Apostle says elsewhere: "Because you are sons, God has sent forth the Spirit of His Son into your hearts, crying out, 'Abba, Father.'" The Christian's God is your Father. And Jesus Christ is your elder brother. Your Father gave you Jesus Christ — the most precious thing He had. So what good thing will He then keep from you? If He's already given what's most precious to Him to you, what good thing is God withholding from you? Whatever you don't have right now is the case because it is good for you not to have it — because if it were good for you to have it right now, God would have given it to you.

Brothers and sisters, despite your sinfulness and your natural hatred toward Him, your heavenly Father changed your heart and put His name on you. He has made you heirs of all things.

## The Father Blesses Us Beyond What We Can Ask or Imagine

Paul prays that God, through Jesus Christ, would do the things that the Father had already promised He would do for those who are His children through faith. The measure of God's blessing is glorious and rich. Look at verse 16: "He would grant you according to the riches of His glory." The resources of God's blessings are nothing less than the infinity of God.

Our Father strengthens us at the deepest level, in verse 16: "to be strengthened with might through His Spirit in the inner man." This strengthening is not a superficial temporary boost — it is the strengthening of the core of who we are, the inner man. If you are losing heart right now, your Father is ready to strengthen you — no strings attached.

Our Father has His Son dwell in us through His Spirit. Verse 17: "that Christ may dwell in your hearts through faith." The proof that Christ dwells in us is that you are becoming more and more like Him. The more sanctified you get, the more like Christ you become, and the more you recognize your own sinfulness — and that's good, because it shows growth in the Lord Jesus Christ.

## The Greatest Blessing: The Love of Christ

Really, the greatest blessing — and the one that fills all others — is that our Father loves us. Verses 17-19: "that you, being rooted and grounded in love, may be able to comprehend with all the saints what is the width and length and depth and height — to know the love of Christ, which passes knowledge, that you may be filled with all the fullness of God."

The Father loves you. Paul prays that the family of God would understand how great the Father's love for us is. It is a paradoxical thing — we are to grasp what we cannot fully understand. You can almost see Paul choking up in talking about the love of Christ. As a commentator put it: "The love of Christ is wide enough to reach the whole world and beyond. It is long enough to stretch from eternity to eternity. It is high enough to raise both Gentiles and Jews to heavenly places in Christ Jesus. It is deep enough to rescue people from sin's degradation and even from the grip of Satan himself."

The love of Christ is the pattern for our love for one another. It always points to the Father's love for us. It has been poured out in our hearts by the Spirit. It is displayed in the death of Jesus while we were still sinners. It brings hope. Nothing can separate us from it. It compels selfless living. It unites us. It purifies and sanctifies us. It adopts us as children of God. It is ultimately declared in the incarnation of the Son.

Ten million years from now, we will not have gotten close to exhausting the love of Christ for us. Ten trillion years from now, we will still be discovering it.

The result, in verse 19, is that we "may be filled with all the fullness of God." God fills His family. God fills you with Himself.

## Conclusion

Life can be extremely difficult, and this season can be tough for some of us — but there is nothing that dispels the darkness of the soul, that lifts the droopy hands, that encourages the fainthearted, like the love of God through Jesus Christ. In a season in which some of you may feel very alone, the reality is that you are not alone. Your Father is ready to fill you to the brim with Himself, and your family — the household of God — is here to love you.

Father, we pray that this truth will be a consolation to our hearts and an encouragement — but also a conviction, that those who don't believe in you in our midst today would come to faith in you, that they would hear about the love that you have for your family and desire to be part of it. We pray that you rule supreme in all our hearts, in Jesus' name. Amen.
